[2026-07-04 20:07:13 INFO] Process design reviewer: **Process design reviewer<->Team leader on : GiveSuggestion, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Problem: In branch `branch11` of `exclusivegateway3`, the activities inside `parallelgateway2` (`activity17`, `activity18`, `activity19`) are placed before the brain CT scan activity `activity20`, which creates an unreasonable order of care.

Suggested modification: Move `activity20` ("perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset") before `parallelgateway2` in `branch11`.

Reason: For patients with high-risk TIA or minor stroke, brain CT is a priority investigation because antiplatelet therapy can only be started after intracranial hemorrhage is excluded. Placing vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests before the CT may imply that these supporting investigations are completed first, which could delay the essential brain imaging and subsequent treatment decision.

Modified scheme:
```xml
<process>
  ...
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
    <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is accessible without delay that poses risk to patient health' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20'/>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch12'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='arrange vascular imaging' objects='vascular imaging studies' id='activity17'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch13'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='initiate heart monitoring' objects='cardiac monitoring' id='activity18'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch14'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='order laboratory tests' objects='laboratory tests' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      ...
    </branch>
    ...
  </exclusiveGateway>
  ...
</process>
```

[2026-07-04 20:07:13 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-07-04 20:07:13 INFO] **[Seminar Conclusion]**:

 Problem: In branch `branch11` of `exclusivegateway3`, the activities inside `parallelgateway2` (`activity17`, `activity18`, `activity19`) are placed before the brain CT scan activity `activity20`, which creates an unreasonable order of care.

Suggested modification: Move `activity20` ("perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset") before `parallelgateway2` in `branch11`.

Reason: For patients with high-risk TIA or minor stroke, brain CT is a priority investigation because antiplatelet therapy can only be started after intracranial hemorrhage is excluded. Placing vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests before the CT may imply that these supporting investigations are completed first, which could delay the essential brain imaging and subsequent treatment decision.

Modified scheme:
```xml
<process>
  ...
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
    <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is accessible without delay that poses risk to patient health' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20'/>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch12'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='arrange vascular imaging' objects='vascular imaging studies' id='activity17'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch13'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='initiate heart monitoring' objects='cardiac monitoring' id='activity18'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch14'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='order laboratory tests' objects='laboratory tests' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      ...
    </branch>
    ...
  </exclusiveGateway>
  ...
</process>
```

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

ED, emergency department.', 'suggestions': 'Problem: In branch branch11 of exclusivegateway3, the activities inside parallelgateway2 (activity17, activity18, activity19) are placed before the brain CT scan activity activity20, which creates an unreasonable order of care.\n\nSuggested modification: Move activity20 ("perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset") before parallelgateway2 in branch11.\n\nReason: For patients with high-risk TIA or minor stroke, brain CT is a priority investigation because antiplatelet therapy can only be started after intracranial hemorrhage is excluded. Placing vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests before the CT may imply that these supporting investigations are completed first, which could delay the essential brain imaging and subsequent treatment decision.\n\nModified scheme:\nxml\n&lt;process&gt;\n  ...\n  &lt;exclusiveGateway id=\'exclusivegateway3\'&gt;\n    &lt;branch condition=\'specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |

[2026-07-04 20:07:13 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='ED team' action='prioritize triage for suspected TIA or minor stroke' objects='suspected TIA or minor stroke patient' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED team' action='notify ED physician or stroke-capable clinician and begin early treatment workflow' objects='suspected TIA or minor stroke patient' id='activity1_2'/>
  <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='blood glucose indicates hypoglycemia' id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED team' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='first random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L' id='branch2'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='repeat blood glucose value' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='blood glucose is not hypoglycemic and first random glucose value is <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
      <activity role='ED team' action='continue glucose management monitoring' objects='blood glucose status' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate body temperature as part of routine vital signs' objects='temperature reading' id='activity6'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C' id='branch4'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
        <branch id='branch5'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='elevated temperature' id='activity7'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch6'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='possible infection source' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch7'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required' objects='antipyretic therapy, antimicrobial therapy' id='activity9'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <activity role='ED team' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours as indicated' objects='temperature readings' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C' id='branch8'>
      <activity role='ED team' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours as indicated' objects='temperature readings' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='ED physician' action='assess TIA or minor stroke risk features, symptom pattern, and symptom onset timing' objects='clinical assessment findings' id='activity12'/>
  <activity role='ED physician' action='assess current antiplatelet use, contraindications to antiplatelet therapy, and swallowing status' objects='medication history, contraindications, swallowing status' id='activity13'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='TIA presents with fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch9'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='evaluate patient in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='high-risk fluctuating or crescendo TIA patient' id='activity14'/>
      <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment' objects='secondary prevention risk factors and care needs' id='activity15'/>
      <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='provide secondary prevention therapies and transition plan for admission or discharge' objects='secondary prevention therapies, follow-up plan, transition plan' id='activity16'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient has high-risk TIA or minor stroke and medical attention is sought within 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch10'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is accessible without delay that poses risk to patient health' id='branch11'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch12'>
              <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='arrange vascular imaging' objects='vascular imaging studies' id='activity17'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch13'>
              <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='initiate heart monitoring' objects='cardiac monitoring' id='activity18'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch14'>
              <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='order laboratory tests' objects='laboratory tests' id='activity19'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
            <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded on brain imaging, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch15'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
                <branch condition='ASA regimen selected' id='branch16'>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch17'>
                      <activity role='clinic physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='activity21'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch18'>
                      <activity role='clinic physician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose' objects='ASA' id='activity22'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                  <activity role='clinic physician' action='prescribe enteric coated ASA daily for maintenance, usually 81 mg/day within the 81 to 325 mg range' objects='enteric coated ASA' id='activity23'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen selected' id='branch19'>
                  <activity role='clinic physician' action='administer clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel' id='activity24'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen selected' id='branch20'>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch21'>
                      <activity role='clinic physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose before starting extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='rectal or gastric ASA, extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA' id='activity25'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch22'>
                      <activity role='clinic physician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose before starting extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='ASA, extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA' id='activity26'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded, antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated, or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch23'>
              <activity role='clinic physician' action='withhold initiation of new antiplatelet therapy and continue tailored stroke-prevention planning' objects='imaging result, contraindications, current antiplatelet therapy' id='activity27'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity28'/>
          <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment' objects='secondary prevention risk factors and care needs' id='activity29'/>
          <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='provide secondary prevention therapies and transition plan for admission or discharge' objects='secondary prevention therapies, follow-up plan, transition plan' id='activity30'/>
        </branch>

          <activity role='ED team' action='perform evaluation in an appropriately resourced ED' objects='high-risk TIA or minor stroke patient' id='activity31'/>
          <activity role='ED team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity32'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
            <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded on brain imaging, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch25'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
                <branch condition='ASA regimen selected' id='branch26'>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
                    <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch27'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='activity33'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch28'>
                      <activity role='ED physician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose' objects='ASA' id='activity34'/>

              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded, antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated, or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch33'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='withhold initiation of new antiplatelet therapy and continue tailored stroke-prevention planning' objects='imaging result, contraindications, current antiplatelet therapy' id='activity39'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission decision' id='activity40'/>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity41'/>
          <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment' objects='secondary prevention risk factors and care needs' id='activity42'/>
          <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='provide secondary prevention therapies and transition plan for admission or discharge' objects='secondary prevention therapies, follow-up plan, transition plan' id='activity43'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient has high-risk TIA or minor stroke and medical attention is sought after 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch34'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient for evaluation at a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic or an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent referral' id='activity44'/>
      <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment' objects='secondary prevention risk factors and care needs' id='activity45'/>
      <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='provide secondary prevention therapies and transition plan for admission or discharge' objects='secondary prevention therapies, follow-up plan, transition plan' id='activity46'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient has TIA without high-risk features' id='branch35'>
      <activity role='ED team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity47'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway12'>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded on brain imaging, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch36'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway13'>
            <branch condition='ASA regimen selected' id='branch37'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway14'>
                <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch38'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='activity48'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch39'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose' objects='ASA' id='activity49'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='prescribe enteric coated ASA daily for maintenance, usually 81 mg/day within the 81 to 325 mg range' objects='enteric coated ASA' id='activity50'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen selected' id='branch40'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='administer clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel' id='activity51'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen selected' id='branch41'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway15'>
                <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or cannot swallow' id='branch42'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose before starting extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='rectal or gastric ASA, extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA' id='activity52'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch43'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose before starting extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='ASA, extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA' id='activity53'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded, antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated, or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch44'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='withhold initiation of new antiplatelet therapy and continue stroke-prevention planning' objects='imaging result, contraindications, current antiplatelet therapy' id='activity54'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity55'/>
      <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment' objects='secondary prevention risk factors and care needs' id='activity56'/>
      <activity role='stroke prevention team' action='provide secondary prevention therapies and transition plan for admission or discharge' objects='secondary prevention therapies, follow-up plan, transition plan' id='activity57'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
</process>
suggestion:
Problem: In branch `branch11` of `exclusivegateway3`, the activities inside `parallelgateway2` (`activity17`, `activity18`, `activity19`) are placed before the brain CT scan activity `activity20`, which creates an unreasonable order of care.

Suggested modification: Move `activity20` ("perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset") before `parallelgateway2` in `branch11`.

Reason: For patients with high-risk TIA or minor stroke, brain CT is a priority investigation because antiplatelet therapy can only be started after intracranial hemorrhage is excluded. Placing vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests before the CT may imply that these supporting investigations are completed first, which could delay the essential brain imaging and subsequent treatment decision.

Modified scheme:
```xml
<process>
  ...
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
    <branch condition='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is accessible without delay that poses risk to patient health' id='branch11'>
      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='perform brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity20'/>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch12'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='arrange vascular imaging' objects='vascular imaging studies' id='activity17'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch13'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='initiate heart monitoring' objects='cardiac monitoring' id='activity18'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch14'>
          <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='order laboratory tests' objects='laboratory tests' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      ...
    </branch>
    ...
  </exclusiveGateway>
  ...
</process>
```
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-07-04 20:07:45 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Process design reviewer on : ProcessReview, turn 0**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |
